Article.ArticleTitle: HCUC: Combining holistic consistency and uniqueness for PET/CT multi-modal segmentation.

Abstract.AbstractText: Multi-modal segmentation aims to harness the complementary properties of different modalities while exploiting their individual strengths for optimal performance on various dimensions. However, existing PET/CT segmentation methods face significant challenges in leveraging complementary multi-modal features effectively. Current solutions often suffer from inadequate feature fusion, over-reliance on single-modal guidance, and simplistic decoding processes. Our proposed HCUC framework addresses these challenges by incorporating holistic consistency and uniqueness, enabling precise segmentation even for complex tumor morphologies. Specifically, we adjust inter- and intra-modal heterogeneity and on this basis capture holistic shared features to facilitate pixel-level multi-modal fusion. Simultaneously, we adaptively extract and filter out conducive unique features through two targeted pre-trained encoders to maximize respective advantages, including high sensitivity of PET and high specificity of CT. Ultimately, through common and unique features combination, we attain segmentation results with both different modality properties and refined modality-specific attributes. Validated on two PET/CT datasets, HCUC achieves state-of-art performance and precise visualizations especially on tricky cases, with DSC of 83.05% on HECKTOR and 82.34% on AutoPET. Furthermore, on MRI dataset BraTS, HCUC surpasses tailored advanced models, with DSC of 94.34%, reflecting strong generalization and scalability. With consistently exceptional performance, HCUC holds huge potential to extend to modalities from diverse fields and to address complex scenarios like real-time dynamic segmentation. Codes will soon be publicly available.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declaration of competing interest The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
